This is when you get to the higher level. Right now, we're just learning basic, nahuwa and saraf, and basic Arabic, right? When you get to the higher level, it's something called balagha, which is rhetoric. Meaning, what is it that makes speech more beautiful? So, now, one concept in balagha, in Arabic rhetoric, is called al-mushakala al-abdiya. The Prophet ﷺ was saying that, I don't worship what you worship, the thing that you worship, which is the idols. So, in that case, maa is justified. Why? Because maa is used for, things that don't have intellect, and idols obviously do not have intellect. So, using maa over there, it makes sense, and it's the way the rule normally goes. But in the context of Allah ﷻ, it's used out of its normal usage, for that purpose of symmetry, for keeping that symmetry. This is something that's, you know, it's a higher concept, but it's just an idea I wanted to explain to you, just to answer the question. title: Understanding the Use of "HathA" and "Zalika" in the Qur'an: Proximity and Context
summary: This session focuses on distinguishing between the Arabic demonstrative pronouns 'HathA' (هذا) and 'ZalikA' (ذلك) using examples from the Quran. The teacher explains how these terms denote proximity ('HathA') and distance ('ZalikA'), both physically and contextually, and their grammatical rules related to gender and number.
key_points: The demonstrative pronoun 'HathA' (هذا) is used for nearby or psychologically proximate objects, masculine singular nouns, and for items with or without intellect when indicating immediacy.
In contrast, 'ZalikA' (ذلك) refers to distant or conceptually remote objects, also masculine singular. It appears in contexts where emphasis on remoteness is needed, even metaphorically.

Quranic examples such as فيSurah Yaseen 36:48 and Surah Al-Kafirun illustrate how these terms maintain grammatical agreement (masculine/feminine, near/far) while serving stylistic purposes like rhyme and balance.
Balagha (Arabic rhetoric) concepts like 'mushakala' explain the Quran's use of repetition and symmetry, guiding students to deeper analysis beyond basic grammar.
questions: [00:02:00] Q: Why does Surah Al-Kafirun use 'maa'? — A: AllAhu ﷻ uses 'maa' for non-intellectual idols, maintaining symmetry despite its usual rule to avoid grammatical errors.
[00:04:00] Q: Can Isa be in Hell as per Quran 15:32? — A: 'maa' refers to non-intellectual objects; since Isa has intellect, it's a misinterpretation.

quiz.q: What are the three grammatical characteristics of هَذَا (Hatha)?
options: Masculine, singular, near
Feminine, singular, near
Masculine, plural, near
Masculine, singular, far
quiz.a: Masculine, singular, near. هَذَا is used for masculine singular nouns that are physically or contextually close to the speaker.

quiz.q: What is the only difference between هَذَا (Hatha) and ذَٰلِكَ (Zalika)?
options: هَذَا is near, ذَٰلِكَ is far — all other properties (masculine, singular) are the same
هَذَا is masculine, ذَٰلِكَ is feminine
هَذَا is singular, ذَٰلِكَ is plural
هَذَا is for intellect, ذَٰلِكَ is for non-intellect
quiz.a: The only difference is proximity: هَذَا is for near objects, ذَٰلِكَ is for far objects. Both are masculine and singular.

quiz.q: When is مَا used instead of مَنْ when asking a question?
options: مَا is for things without intellect (objects, animals); مَنْ is for things with intellect (people)
مَا is for singular; مَنْ is for plural
مَا is for masculine; مَنْ is for feminine
They are interchangeable
quiz.a: مَا is used to ask about things without intellect (e.g., objects, animals), while مَنْ is used to ask about things that have intellect (e.g., people).

quiz.q: Why does Surah Al-Kafirun use مَا when referring to Allah, even though Allah has intellect?
options: Because of the rhetorical device al-Mushakala — maintaining symmetry with the مَا used earlier for idols
It is a grammatical error in the Quran
Because مَا can always be used for Allah
Because the Prophet ﷺ is speaking, not Allah
quiz.a: This is the rhetorical device called al-Mushakala (المشاكلة), a concept from Balagha (Arabic rhetoric). The word مَا was already used for the idols (which have no intellect), and to preserve the symmetry and beauty of the speech, مَا was used again. The Quran considers both meaning and sound.

quiz.q: Surah Al-Baqarah 2:2 uses ذَٰلِكَ الْكِتَابُ for the Quran even though the Quran is right in front of the reader. Why?
options: To indicate the Quran's elevated status — it is so exalted it is as if it is far above us
Because the Quran was revealed far away in Makkah
It was a mistake by the scribes
ذَٰلِكَ can also mean 'this' in certain dialects
quiz.a: The scholars of Tafsir explain that ذَٰلِكَ here signals the Quran's lofty status — it is of such a high rank that it is as if it is above and beyond us, not merely 'in front of us.' This is a beautiful example of how proximity in Arabic can be metaphorical, not just physical.

quiz.q: Can هَذَا and ذَٰلِكَ be used for things with intellect (like people) or only for objects?
options: Both can be used for anything — people, animals, or objects. The intellect rule only applies to مَا and مَنْ.
هَذَا is only for objects, ذَٰلِكَ is only for people
Neither can be used for people
Only ذَٰلِكَ can be used for people
quiz.a: هَذَا and ذَٰلِكَ can be used for anything — people, animals, or objects. The distinction between intellect (عاقل) and non-intellect (غير عاقل) only applies to مَنْ and مَا.

quran_examples.translation: So let them worship the Lord of this House.
quran_examples.note: هَذَا is used because the Quraysh lived in Makkah — the Kaaba was literally right in front of them every day. This surah was revealed in Makkah (Makki), making the use of هَذَا (near) perfectly fitting.

quran_examples.translation: O my Lord, make this land a place of security.
quran_examples.note: Ibrahim ﷺ made this dua while standing in the valley of Makkah, pointing to the very land beneath his feet. هَذَا fits perfectly: the land is masculine (بَلَد), singular, and physically near — he is literally standing in it.

Allah swears by Makkah using هَذَا because this is a Makki surah — it was revealed in Makkah, making the city contextually near. هَذَا الْبَلَدِ الْأَمِينِ is the answer to Ibrahim's dua: Allah did make it a secure land.

quran_examples.arabic: مَتَىٰ هَٰذَا الْوَعْدُ إِن كُنتُمْ صَادِقِينَ
quran_examples.transliteration: Matā hādhā al-waʿdu in kuntum ṣādiqīn
quran_examples.translation: When is this promise, if you are truthful?
quran_examples.note: The disbelievers use هَذَا for the Day of Judgment even though it hasn't happened yet — because the Prophet ﷺ was constantly warning them about it, making it psychologically close and present in their minds. Proximity in Arabic is not always physical.

quran_examples.translation: That is the Book about which there is no doubt.
quran_examples.note: Although the Quran is right in front of the reader, Allah uses ذَٰلِكَ (far) to indicate the Quran's exalted status — it is of such a lofty rank that it is as if it is far above us. This is one of the most discussed examples in Tafsir literature.

quran_examples.transliteration: Wa lā antum ʿābidūna mā aʿbud
quran_examples.translation: Nor will you be worshippers of what I worship.
quran_examples.note: The word مَا is used here for what the Prophet ﷺ worships, i.e. Allah — even though Allah has intellect (which normally calls for مَنْ). This is the rhetorical device al-Mushakala: مَا was used just before for the idols (correct usage), and is repeated here to maintain symmetry and beauty of sound in the verse.
